text: Although I'm sharing an RTAP for a right lower quadrant defect clinically on my exam, as well as on a pre-op CAT scan that I had gotten,
The surgeon visually examines the surgical field, identifying a large indirect hernia defect on the right side.
The surgeon creates a wide peritoneal flap, similar to the setup for an inguinal hernia repair, exposing the underlying structures for further dissection.
Not that it changed too much of the surgical plan, whether this is a Spigelian or an inguinal or a combination of both.
I typically do close Spigelian defects. This I did not close because it was more of in the indirect space and I didn't want to risk any trapment of some of the nerves.
The surgeon identifies a true obturator hernia on the patient's side, noting the presence of a peritoneal sac that will be covered by the planned mesh placement.
The surgeon reduces the entire hernia sac, with the round ligament clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum, lifting it away from underlying structures to safely cauterize and divide the tissue.
The surgeon completes the parietalization of the right medial peritoneal overlay (MPO), ensuring the peritoneum is adequately separated from underlying structures.
The surgeon approaches the obturator space, revealing a visible hernia defect in the area.
The surgeon visually identifies the peritoneal sac extending into the right obturator space, indicating the presence of a hernia defect.
Again, this didn't change much of the repair. Typically this is just a fat plug and not with a true peritoneal sac.
The surgeon completes the preparation of the surgical site, creating space for the placement of the mesh in the hernia repair area.
The surgeon checks for a lipoma in the indirect space, observing a small amount of fat extending into the area.
The camera provides a clear view of the hernia defect, revealing a widened indirect inguinal ring.
The surgeon positions a fifteen by fifteen pro grip mesh to cover the hernia defect, ensuring it adequately spans the space previously exposed.
The surgeon ensures that the obturator space is adequately covered, likely with the mesh being placed to secure the area.
The surgeon closes the flap over the prepared space, completing the setup for mesh placement in the hernia repair.
